summary.en: The undeniable heart of Staunton, this district is a living museum of late-19th-century architecture with an arts-forward and bustling vibe.
A quiet, historic residential neighborhood perched high above the city, offering a peaceful, contemplative atmosphere and sweeping views.

name.en: Gypsy Hill Park & The West End

summary.en: Sprawling and green, this area is the city’s communal backyard, a place where locals come to play, relax, and reflect on history.

name.en: The By & By Café and Beer Garden

description.en: A true local's hub with a relaxed, bohemian atmosphere, known for its coffee, craft beer, and a secluded, leafy back patio.
A breathtaking, non-profit recreation of Shakespeare's indoor theatre that serves as a working playhouse and a transportive cultural landmark.
An iconic downtown landmark and the perfect starting point for a scavenger hunt to spot the nearly 200 buildings by architect T.J. Collins.
A simple, thought-provoking piece of public art on the side of the iconic Clocktower that speaks to the city’s creative spirit.
The neighborhood's crown jewel, this small park offers a panoramic vista of historic downtown, the surrounding hills, and the train lines.
A private 1840s residence modified by Jedediah Hotchkiss, famed cartographer for the Confederate Army, making it a fascinating landmark to view from the street.
An elegant, Jefferson-inspired Federal-style home from the 18th century whose stately presence predates the surrounding Victorian boom.
A classic 214-acre American public park with a charmingly retro feel, serving as a hub of local life and recreation.
A central feature of the park, perfect for a leisurely walk and capturing reflections of the Victorian-era bandstand on a calm day.
A dose of pure nostalgia, this miniature train offers charming rides through the park and is a delight for all ages.
Formerly the city's segregated high school, this building is a crucial part of Staunton's story and a place to reflect on the Civil Rights era.
Opened in 1950 as the only park for African-Americans before desegregation, its 148 acres offer a deeper understanding of the city's past.
A key building within Montgomery Hall Park, named for a local community leader.

prompt.en: Look up! Find the most ornate Victorian building facade and capture a photo of its intricate details.
Discover a hidden alley or narrow side street and take a photo that captures its unique character.
Take a selfie that shows off the neighborhood's creative vibe, with public art or a colorful storefront in the background.
Search the old brick buildings for a 'ghost sign'—a faded, hand-painted advertisement from a bygone era—and snap a picture.
Find a local cafe and take a mouth-watering photo of a pastry or coffee creation through the window.
Point your camera upwards and frame a shot of the sky using the historic rooftops and cornices as a geometric border.
Strike a dramatic pose like a character from a Shakespearean tragedy or comedy on a historic street corner.
Find a unique architectural detail—an ornate window arch, a metal cresting, or a whimsical turret—and make it the subject of your photo.
Examine the base of the buildings until you find one with a date carved into its cornerstone. Photograph this piece of history.
Find a menu board outside a local restaurant and take a picture of the most interesting-sounding dish.
Capture a reflection of a historic building in a modern shop window or a puddle after rain.
If you spot a busker or street performer, capture a respectful photo of them adding to the city's artistic soundtrack.
Find the best panoramic overlook of downtown Staunton and take a sweeping landscape photo.
Photograph a grand, historic home that looks like it has a hundred years of stories to tell.
Find a quiet bench or peaceful spot and take a selfie that captures the calm, contemplative vibe of the neighborhood.
Many homes here have beautiful gardens. Find one visible from the street and photograph your favorite flower or plant.
Use a wrought-iron fence, a gate, or tree branches as a natural frame for a photo of a historic house or the view.
This is a vertical neighborhood! Find the steepest-looking street or staircase and take a photo that emphasizes the incline.
Find a house with a unique or brightly colored front door and make it the star of your photograph.
If you're here in the late afternoon, capture a photo of the warm, golden light on the historic homes and long shadows on the streets.
Look for a historical plaque on a building or in a park. Take a photo of it and learn a new fact about the area.
Get up close and personal with the neighborhood's history. Take a detailed photo of an old brick sidewalk, a stone wall, or peeling paint on a porch.
Many homes feature beautiful porches. Capture a photo of an inviting-looking porch, complete with rocking chairs or hanging plants.
Take a selfie with the entire city of Staunton laid out behind you from a high vantage point.
Capture a candid photo of local life: a baseball game, a family picnic, or friends walking by the pond.
Find an empty park bench and take an artistic photo of it, imagining the stories it could tell.
Take a selfie while enjoying a moment of peace and quiet on the park's sprawling green lawns.
Find a spot that speaks to the area's history and take a thoughtful photo. Try using a black and white filter to evoke the past.
On a calm day, capture a perfect reflection of trees, the bandstand, or the sky in the park's duck pond.
Photograph something that feels charmingly retro or nostalgic, like the mini-train or the old-fashioned bandstand.
Find a walking path or trail and take a photo looking down its length, using it as a leading line in your composition.
Pose on a swing or slide like you're a kid again for a fun, playful selfie.
Search for a monument, memorial, or dedication plaque within the park. Photograph it and read its inscription.
Find the most interesting or majestic tree in the park and take a photo that captures its age and character.
Listen for the unique sounds of the park—a train whistle, music from the bandstand, cheering from a game—and capture a photo of the source.
Take a picture of the local wildlife. The ducks at the pond are a great place to start!
